action.thought_summary: The initial requirement mentions multiple users interacting simultaneously with multimedia content. I need to understand the physical setup and interaction model first, as this fundamentally shapes the system design.
action.question: When you say multiple users interact simultaneously, what is the physical setup envisioned—is this a single large shared touchscreen/table that several people gather around, or multiple separate stations that each user operates independently?

action.thought_summary: The user wants multiple independent stations for museum visitors. I need to probe interaction details, content specifics, and UI/style preferences. Starting with how users interact with 3D models since that's the most complex interaction type mentioned.
action.question: For the 3D models, what types of interactions should users be able to perform—such as rotating, zooming, exploding/cross-section views, or triggering animations—and are there any physical constraints like the stations using touchscreens vs. mouse/keyboard input?

action.thought_summary: The user asked for a more specific question about input constraints. In a museum context, the input method significantly affects interaction design. I should ask about the physical form factor and input peripherals they're considering for these stations.
action.question: Since this is for a museum setting, are you envisioning each station as a touchscreen kiosk (like a tablet or vertical touchscreen display) where users interact directly with their fingers, or a seated workstation with a traditional monitor plus mouse and keyboard? This affects how we design the zoom and rotate gestures—for example, pinch-to-zoom on touch vs. scroll-wheel zoom with a mouse.

action.thought_summary: User has specified separate stations, zoom/rotate interactions, and color preferences (sky blue background, navy elements). Input method is undecided. I should probe other uncovered areas like content organization, navigation workflows, or session management which are critical for a museum system.
action.question: How should the multimedia content be organized and navigated—for example, should items be grouped by exhibit theme, time period, or artifact type, and should users be able to search or filter content, or is it a fixed curated sequence they browse through?

action.thought_summary: User has given visual preferences (sky blue background, navy elements) and basic interaction needs (zoom, rotate) but hasn't specified details about how different content types are displayed or session behavior. I'll probe about text content display and session management since these are concrete interaction details relevant to museums.
action.question: For the text content displayed at each station, should users be able to scroll through long articles, or should text be presented in bite-sized chunks with next/previous navigation? Also, should each station automatically reset to a home screen after a period of inactivity so the next museum visitor starts fresh?

action.thought_summary: The user has expressed strong visual preferences (sky blue background, navy elements) but is uncertain about many functional aspects. They've asked me to focus on visual design or interaction details. I'll probe a specific interaction detail: how selecting and examining an item works—whether it opens in a dedicated view or stays inline—since this affects both interaction flow and visual layout.
action.question: When a user selects an image or 3D model to examine in detail, should it expand into a full-screen immersive view with zoom/rotate controls overlaid, or should the detail view appear alongside a content list so users can easily browse between items without going back?

action.thought_summary: The user has expressed strong visual design preferences (sky blue background, navy elements) but has been uncertain about many functional aspects. I should probe the visual design area where they've shown confidence, asking about specific UI elements and their styling to uncover more implicit requirements.
action.question: You mentioned preferring navy interface elements—should this navy color apply to interactive elements like buttons and navigation bars, or also to content panels and borders? Additionally, should text displayed on the sky blue background be in navy as well, or do you prefer a contrasting color like white or dark gray for readability?

action.thought_summary: The user has been uncertain about many aspects but showed strong preferences on visual design (sky blue background, navy elements). They keep redirecting me to ask about visual design or interaction details. Let me probe a more specific interaction detail - perhaps about whether there should be transition animations when switching between items, or about audio since 'multimedia' was mentioned but only images/text/3D discussed.
action.question: You mentioned the system should handle images, text, and 3D models—should it also support audio content such as narration clips or sound effects that play when a user selects an exhibit item, or is the system limited to visual content only?

action.thought_summary: User has deflected many broad questions but gave two concrete preferences (sky blue background, navy elements). Need to try very specific, concrete questions about practical deployment constraints that might trigger definite preferences. Target audience age and accessibility is a concrete museum concern.
action.question: Should the interface accommodate young children (e.g., larger touch targets, simpler navigation, minimal text) or is it designed primarily for adult visitors, and do you need to meet any accessibility standards such as WCAG for visitors with visual or motor impairments?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on many topics but expressed strong preferences for sky blue background and navy elements, and directed me toward visual design or interaction details. I should ask a concrete question about the home screen layout or navigation structure since that's a fundamental visual/interaction design decision.
action.question: When a visitor first approaches a station, what should they see on the home screen—should it display a grid of thumbnail images representing the exhibit items, a single featured item with a 'start exploring' prompt, or a list of category buttons they can tap to browse by topic?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most questions but expressed strong preferences about visual design (sky blue background, navy elements) and confirmed zoom/rotate interactions. They keep asking for more specific questions about visual design or interaction details. I should probe a very specific interaction detail about the zoom/rotate behavior they already confirmed wanting.
action.question: For the zoom and rotate interaction you mentioned, should there be visible on-screen controls (like +/− buttons and a circular rotation dial) that users can tap, or should the zoom and rotate work purely through gestures like pinch-to-zoom and two-finger rotate, with no visible control buttons on screen?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most questions but expressed strong preferences for sky blue background and navy interface elements, and keeps asking for more specific questions about visual design or interaction details. I should ask a very specific, narrow question about visual design that they might actually answer.
action.question: Should each station display the museum's logo or name, and if so, should it appear as a persistent header bar across the top of the screen at all times, or only on the home/landing screen?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most questions but expressed strong preferences about visual design (sky blue background, navy elements). I should try one more very specific visual/interaction question before considering finishing, since they keep asking for questions about visual design or interaction details.
action.question: When a user taps or clicks on an exhibit item to select it, should there be a visual highlight effect—such as a navy border appearing around the selected item, or the item slightly enlarging—to clearly indicate which item is currently active?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most questions but keeps suggesting I ask about visual design or interaction details. I'll try a very concrete, specific visual design question about button styling since they mentioned navy elements.
action.question: For the navy-colored interactive elements like buttons, should they have rounded corners for a softer, modern feel, or sharp square corners for a more formal, traditional museum aesthetic?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most design and interaction details, only confirming: separate stations, zoom/rotate functionality, sky blue background, and navy interface elements. I should try probing operational/technical requirements instead of design details, as these may yield more concrete answers.
action.question: How many separate stations do you expect to deploy simultaneously in the museum, and should the multimedia content (images, text, 3D models) be updatable remotely by museum staff without restarting the system, or is it acceptable to load content once and update it manually during maintenance?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most questions but expressed strong preferences only about sky blue background and navy interface elements. They keep requesting more specific questions about visual design or interaction details. I'll try a very concrete, specific visual design question about typography that might elicit a definitive preference.
action.question: For the text displayed on the sky blue background, should the system use a serif font (like Times New Roman) for a traditional, classic museum feel, or a sans-serif font (like Helvetica) for a modern, clean appearance?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most design decisions but confirmed sky blue background and navy interface elements. I should try one more specific question about an aspect not yet covered—transition animations or loading behavior for 3D models—before considering finishing the interview.
action.question: When a 3D model is loading at a station, should the system display a loading indicator—such as a navy spinning circle or progress bar—so visitors know the content is loading rather than thinking the screen is frozen?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deferred on most design and interaction questions, only confirming: separate independent stations, zoom/rotate capability, sky blue background, and navy interface elements. With only 2 turns remaining and the user repeatedly saying they need to consider further, I should try one more targeted question about a concrete visual design detail before finishing.
action.question: Should the navy buttons and interactive elements have a hover or press effect—such as becoming slightly lighter or darker when a visitor touches or clicks them—to provide visual feedback that the system registered their action?

action.thought_summary: After 19 turns, the user has consistently deferred most design and feature decisions. The only firm requirements elicited are: separate independent stations, zoom/rotate for 3D models, sky blue background, and navy interface elements. Nearly all other questions about interaction details, content organization, accessibility, input methods, and UI specifics have been met with uncertainty. It's time to finish the interview.
action.finish_summary: Elicited requirements for the interactive multimedia display system for museums: (1) Multiple separate stations where each user operates independently without interference from other users. (2) Supports images, text, and 3D models. (3) Users can zoom and rotate content to examine multimedia items in detail. (4) Visual design: sky blue background with navy-colored interface elements. Numerous design decisions remain undecided by the user, including: input method (touchscreen vs mouse/keyboard), content organization and navigation structure, text display format, detail view layout, home screen design, on-screen controls vs gesture-based interaction, museum branding placement, selection highlight effects, button corner styling, font choice, loading indicators, hover/press feedback effects, number of stations, content update mechanism, audio support, target audience age range, accessibility standards compliance, and auto-reset behavior.
